experiments.reasoning: The passage describes a deliberate observation of a mushroom with specific characteristics (size, shape, appearance, and behavior when cut), including a systematic description of its properties (milky juice, taste, texture, and reaction to drying). The author also notes the presence of fly maggots in the mushrooms, which suggests a controlled observation of their biological state. The mention of measurements (e.g., stalk height and thickness) and qualitative observations (e.g., juice properties) aligns with experimental methodology.
experiments.description: Mr. Lister observed a large, round mushroom with a thick, fleshy footstalk and open gills underneath. When cut, the mushroom released a white, milk-like juice. The author noted several properties of this juice, including its spicy, biting taste, lack of clamminess, and tendency to congeal and dry into a firm cake. Additionally, the mushrooms were found to be infested with fly maggots, suggesting a study of their biological and chemical properties.
experiments.quote: In these Woods I then found an infinite number of Mushrooms, some wither'd, and others new-sprung and flourishing. They were of a large size, something bigger than the Ordinary red-gilled eatable Mushroom or Champignon, and very much of their shape... If you cut any part of this Mushroom, it will bleed exceeding freely a Milk-white Juice, concerning which note; 1. That this Milky Juice tastes much hotter upon the tongue than Pepper. 2. That it is not clammy to the touch. 3. That the Air does not much discolour it, or the blade of a knife; as is usual with most Vegetable Juices. 4. That it became in the glass-viol, I drew it into, suddenly concret and stiff, and did in some daies dry into a firm Cake. 5. That it then also, when well dried, retain'd its fierce biting tast and white colour.
experiments.outcome: The passage does not explicitly state the outcome of the experiment. However, the author notes several observable properties of the mushroom and its juice, including its chemical behavior (concretion and drying) and taste characteristics. The presence of fly maggots suggests a biological observation, but no explicit conclusion is drawn.
